coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: Cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Atchudan, Raji
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
Shim, Jae-Jin
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: A fast and facile microwave approach for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is reported. The N-CDs were hydrothermally synthesized using l -ascorbic acid (AA) and β-alanine (BA) as the carbon precursor and the nitrogen dopant, respectively. The morphology of synthesized N-CDs was characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and the elemental composition was analyzed using elemental mapping method. The crystallinity and graphitation of N-CDs were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The doping of nitrogen over the carbon dots (CDs) was revealed by attenuated total reflection conjunction with Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were examined by UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs emit strong blue fluorescence at 401nm under excitation of 325nm. The excitation dependent emission property of synthesized N-CDs was exposed from fluorescence results. The quantum yield of synthesized N-CDs is about 14% against the reference quinine sulfate. The cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CDs on Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) and HeLa cells were evaluated through Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) cytotoxicity assay. The results implied that the fluorescent N-CDs showed less cytotoxicity, further which was successfully applied as a staining probe for the confocal imaging of MDCK and HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
N-doped carbon dots
Carbonization
Fluorescence
Cytotoxicity
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) Cell Viability Assay and Cell Imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIP) ( NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391 ) and Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs.
(a) HR-TEM image of the synthesized N-CDs at 20nm magnification, (b) HR-TEM image of synthesized N-CDs at 5nm magnification.

(a) HAADF-STEM image of the synthesized N-CDs, (b) elemental mapping of carbon in synthesized N-CDs, (c) elemental mapping of nitrogen in the synthesized N-CDs, (d) elemental mapping of oxygen in the synthesized N-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern of synthesized N-CDs, (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized N-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the synthesized N-CDs, AA and BA.
(a) XPS full scan survey spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs, (b) high resolution XP spectrum of C1s, (c) high resolution XP spectrum of N1s, (d) high resolution XP spectrum of O1s.
A proposed formation mechanism of N-CDs from AA and BA.

(a) UV–Vis spectrum of synthesized N-CDs, (b) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of synthesized N-CDs, (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs at different wavelengths from 270nm to 325nm, (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs at different wavelengths from 325nm to 350nm.
Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of N-CDs on MDCK and HeLa cells.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of MDCK cells without N-CDs as a control and with N-CDs.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of the HeLa cells without N-CDs as a control and with N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: Cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 712-749, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Gandhigram Rural Institute Deemed University, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and facile microwave approach for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is reported. The N-CDs were hydrothermally synthesized using l -ascorbic acid (AA) and β-alanine (BA) as the carbon precursor and the nitrogen dopant, respectively. The morphology of synthesized N-CDs was characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and the elemental composition was analyzed using elemental mapping method. The crystallinity and graphitation of N-CDs were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. The doping of nitrogen over the carbon dots (CDs) was revealed by attenuated total reflection conjunction with Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were examined by UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs emit strong blue fluorescence at 401nm under excitation of 325nm. The excitation dependent emission property of synthesized N-CDs was exposed from fluorescence results. The quantum yield of synthesized N-CDs is about 14% against the reference quinine sulfate. The cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CDs on Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) and HeLa cells were evaluated through Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) cytotoxicity assay. The results implied that the fluorescent N-CDs showed less cytotoxicity, further which was successfully applied as a staining probe for the confocal imaging of MDCK and HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are materials with at least one dimension in the size range of 1 to 100nm, they have many unique mechanical, electrical, magnetic, optical, and chemical properties compared to the bulk materials [1–5]. Carbon dots (CDs) are the new class of carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10nm [6–8]. CDs were synthesized by laser ablation, electro oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, vapor deposition, and microwave synthesis [9–11]. Among these strategies, the microwave synthesis has become popular because it is rapid and facile [12,13]. Recently, photoluminescent CDs have attracted the attention of researchers owing to its applicability in the sensor and bio-imaging fields [14–16]. Moreover, CDs are highly stable in UV–Vis light and do not undergo photo bleaching [17,18]. Nowadays, green and environment benign sources acts as a better candidate for the production of luminescent CDs. Most of the food sources, fruits and vegetables containing citric acid and tannic acid were utilized as a carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs by hydro-thermal carbonization method [19–25]. The luminescent properties of CDs are strongly depends on the size, dopant, nature of the passivating agent, pH, and solvent [26–28]. Doping of nitrogen, boron and sulfur with CDs results in high fluorescence intensity at different wavelengths [29–31]. These incredible properties of CDs make them applicable to live cell imaging, biology, materials, catalysis, optics, and sensors.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is also known as vitamin C (C6H8O6), which is one of the major phytoconstituents of citrus and berry fruits such as oranges, lemon, grapes, strawberries and raspberries, they are inexpensive and easily available [32]. In addition, AA contains 30.3% of carbon and it is readily soluble in water. β-alanine (BA) is also one of the naturally occurring amino acid. Amino acids are proved as a better nitrogen dopant for the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots [33].

Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) epithelial cells are originated in 1958, from the kidney tissue of an adult female cocker spaniel. MDCK cells have been widely utilized as a general model for epithelial cells in the cytotoxicity analysis and fluorescence imaging [34]. The HeLa cells are oldest and most commonly used human uterine cervical carcinoma cell line in biomedical research, which is isolated from Henrietta Lacks (American cervix cancer patient). Owing to the durability and prolific nature, HeLa cells were served as a model for developing of vaccines, cancer research and confocal imaging [35].

This paper reports the facile one-step synthesis of fluorescent N-CDs by the microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of AA and BA as a carbon and nitrogen source, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with elemental mapping analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, attenuated total reflection conjunction with Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of synthesized N-CDs are examined by UV–Visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CDs was tested on MDCK and HeLa Cells. Further, the N-CDs were applied as a staining probe for the confocal cell imaging of MDCK and HeLa Cells.
AA and BA were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, South Korea and used as received. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

3g of AA was mixed with 1g of BA in 30mL of DI water. The mixture was then transferred to a 100mL Teflon equipped vessel and placed in a MARS-6 microwave reaction system (900W power) with a ramping time of 5min for 180°C and a holding at the same temperature for 1h. After reaction, the vessel was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The obtained mixture was ultra-centrifuged with 15,000rpm for 15min to separate the larger sized carbon particles from N-CDs. The larger sized carbon particles settled at the bottom of centrifuge tube and the N-CDs containing the supernatant solution was removed carefully for dialysis. The dialysis process was performed using a cellulose acetate membrane (1000MW cutoff) in DI water for 24h. After dialysis, the obtained N-CDs were used for characterization and applications. Fig. 1 presents a schematic diagram on the synthesis of N-CDs.

The synthesized N-CDs were drop coated onto a carbon coated copper disc for HR-TEM analysis. After drying, the images were taken using a FEI-Tecnai TF-20 transmission electron microscope operating at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Elemental mapping was also performed using the same instrument.

For XRD analysis, the N-CDs were drop coated onto a 1×1cm2 dimensioned glass plate and the analysis was performed using PANalytical X'Pert3 MRD instrument using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54Å) operating at 40kV and 30mA.
The Raman spectrum was recorded using a thermo scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer at a laser excitation line of 532nm.
The doping and surface functionalization of synthesized N-CDs was investigated by K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific) XPS analyzer and also by ATR-FTIR (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two) spectroscopic techniques.

The UV–Vis and fluorescence measurements of the N-CDs were carried out using Optizen 3220UV and Hitachi-7000F spectrometer, respectively. For the fluorescence measurements, the excitation wavelength was varied from a lower wavelength to a higher wavelength to find the maximum luminescence intensity. The slit width was fixed to 5nm and the scan speed was set to 240nm/min. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated according to previous report [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) Cell Viability Assay and Cell Imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
The MDCK and HeLa cells were obtained from the Korean cell line bank (South Korea). The CCK-8 cytotoxicity assay was performed on MDCK cells and HeLa cells with different concentrations of synthesized N-CDs. Both the MDCK and HeLa cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a density of 5×103 cells per well. Further, the synthesized N-CDs of various concentrations were added into the wells and incubated for 24h for culture which contains 10% of fetal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin-streptomycin mixture. Further, the cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2. After 24h, 10μL of CCK-8 solution (Dojindo laboratories, Kumamoto, Japan) were added in the each well and incubated for 1h. After 1h, the absorbance was measured at 450nm using a microplate reader (Bio-rad). The % cell viability was calculated according to the following Eq. (1).
(1)
Cell
viability
%
=
Ab
s
s
Ab
s
c
× 100
where, Abs(s) and Abs(c) are the absorbance of sample and absorbance of control, respectively.

The MDCK and HeLa cells were maintained in a growth medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics (penicillin-streptomycin). The cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2. For bio imaging, the MDCK and HeLa cells were seeded in a confocal dish for 24h. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with the synthesized N-CDs for 3h and washed three times with water for 5min each. The cells were immediately viewed under a fully-motorized and automated inverted Olympus microscope system IX81.

The fluorescent N-CDs were prepared by the microwave digestion of AA and BA at 180°C and the formation of CDs were visually identified by the color change from colorless to blackish brown [37]. The as-prepared CDs underwent ultra-centrifugation followed by membrane dialysis. The final solution containing the fluorescent N-CDs was stored in a refrigerator at 10°C for further analysis.

Fig. 2 presents HR-TEM images of the synthesized N-CDs. From the Fig. 2a, it can be seen that the N-CDs were embedded in flake-like carbon particles. The lattice fringes were clearly seen in the high resolution images of N-CDs and were marked by red dotted circles (Fig. 2b). The calculated interlayer distance (the distance between two parallel lattice fringes) was approximately 0.21nm, which corresponds to the C(100) plane of graphitized carbon. The calculated results were consistent with previous reports [38,39]. Fig. 3 shows the high angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images and the elemental mapping of the synthesized N-CDs, which revealed the presence of C, N and O in the synthesized N-CDs.

Fig. 4 depicts the XRD and Raman spectrum of synthesized N-CDs. The Fig. 4a presents the wide-angle XRD pattern of N-CDs. The obtained pattern was compared with the standard diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon. The N-CDs showed broad 2θ patterns at around 23.7 and 42° which was assigned to disordered carbon atoms with a high degree of C(002) and C(100) planes for the hexagonal graphite structure (N-CDs) [40]. The d-spacing of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated using Bragg's Eq. (1) based on the position of the C(002)/C(100) reflection peak.
(2)
n λ = 2 dsin θ
or
d =
n λ
2 sin θ

where, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (λ=1.54Å) and θ is the position of the C(002)/C(100) peak. The d-spacing of N-CDs was approximately 0.37 and 0.21nm for the C(002) and C(100) peaks, respectively. On the other hand, the XRD peak of the N-CDs was broadened due to the small sized N-CDs.

Raman spectroscopy is the one of the most powerful tools for characterizing the carbon related nanomaterials. Fig. 4b shows the Raman spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs. The spectrum showed two different Raman bands at 1365 and 1595cm−1, which corresponds to sp2 carbon (G-band) together with sp3 defects (D-band) in the synthesized N-CDs [41]. The D-band is related to vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The G-band was assigned to the E2g mode of graphite, which is related to the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The peak intensities of the D-band and G-band were denoted as I
D and I
G , respectively. In general, a D: G intensity ratio (ID <IG) less than 1 indicates that the synthesized carbon materials are well graphitized [42]. Furthermore, the I
D /I
G ratio for the synthesized N-CDs was measured to be 0.77, indicating the presence of less defective carbon structures in the synthesized N-CDs. The fluorescence of the synthesized N-CDs is associated with surface defects of carbon atoms that trap the excited-state energy [43]. Therefore, the Raman result suggests that the synthesized N-CDs have a graphitic structure without major surface defects.

Fig. 5 shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of the N-CDs and its precursors, AA and BA. In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs, a broad absorption band between 3200 and 2500cm−1 suggests that the stretching vibrations of −NH and −OH functional groups are interconnected by a hydrogen bond in N-CDs [44]. The sharp bands at 1720, 1370 and 1214cm−1 were assigned to the stretching of −CO (carbonyl), bending of −OH (hydroxyl) and stretching of −C–O (acid) groups, respectively. The small broad bands at 1050cm−1 originates from the stretching of C–N groups in N-CDs. Therefore, the FT-IR study confirmed the presence of amine, acid and hydroxyl moieties on the surface of the synthesized N-CDs.

Fig. 6 presents the XPS survey scan spectrum of the N-CDs and high resolution XPS spectra of C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s). The survey spectrum of N-CDs revealed three apparent binding peaks at 533.08, 400.08 and 285.08eV (Fig. 6a) corresponding to the O (1s), N (1s) and C (1s), respectively. The deconvolution spectrum of C (1s) (Fig. 6b) revealed four peaks at 284.46, 285.82, 287.29, and 288.57eV, corresponding to the CC (sp2), C–N, O–CO and CO/CN groups, respectively [45]. The high resolution N (1s) spectrum (Fig. 6c) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.57 and 401.41eV, corresponding to C-NH2 and ON–C functional groups. The XP spectrum of O (1s) (Fig. 6d) was deconvoluted to two peaks at 531.68 and 532.96eV, indicating the presence of −C–O/−N–O and −CO/−NO functional groups [46–49]. Therefore, the XPS data suggests that the synthesized N-CDs had been passivated by amine (C-NH2), keto (−CO) and carboxyl (O–CO) groups. In addition, the calculated atomic percentage of C, O and N in the synthesized N-CDs are 72.16, 23.97 and 3.87% respectively, which confirmed the doping of nitrogen along with oxygen in the synthesized CDs.

The formation mechanism of N-CDs has been proposed and represented in Fig. 7 . The formation of N-CDs from AA and BA can be described in terms of four different stages include polymerization, aromatization, nucleation, and growth. Initially, the inter and intra molecular dehydration takes place between AA and BA, which leads to the formation of bigger sized polymeric particles at above 100°C. Further, while increasing of temperature and time, aromatization of bigger sized polymers will occur, then consequently aromatic clusters are formed at this stage. Further, the formed aromatic clusters undergo the nucleation burst and leads to the formation of N-CDs after reaching the critical supersaturation point [50,51].

UV–Visible spectroscopy is a valuable tool for analyzing the optical properties of nanomaterials. Fig. 8 represents the optical properties of synthesized N-CDs. Fig. 8a and Fig. 8b shows the UV–Vis and excitation spectrum of the N-CDs, respectively. The dilute solution of N-CDs absorbed strongly at 265 and 323nm in the UV–Vis spectrum. The absorbance of N-CDs was attributed to the π-π* transition and n-π* transition of the non-bonding electrons of the nitrogen moiety or carbonyl group passivating the carbon materials, respectively [52].

Fig. 8c and Fig. 8d show the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. The emission spectra of N-CDs were observed in the blue region at approximately 401nm and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs increased. In contrast, no obvious shift in the fluorescence peak was observed after increasing the excitation wavelength from 275 to 325nm. At higher energy excitation (275nm) of N-CDs gives the same emission of lower energy (325nm) excitation, but the intensity was low as compared with lower energy excitation. Therefore, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be derived from the radiative recombination of the excited nonbonding electrons from the n–π* transitions of CO and nitrogen moiety present in the defective N-CDs [53,43]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing excitation wavelength from 325 to 350nm and the emission peaks were red shifted from 400 to 450nm, which is common for most N-CDs [54]. The calculated quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs is 14% against quinine sulfate as reference fluorescence compound; in addition this value is higher than the other reported CDs [3]. The high quantum yield is probably due to the doping and passivating of nitrogen on the CDs surface. Therefore, the broad ranges of the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs are appropriate candidates for bioimaging and biolabeling.

The CCK-8 cell viability assay was performed over different concentrations of synthesized N-CDs on MDCK and HeLa cells. The % cell viability results were shown in Fig. 9 , which suggested that the synthesized N-CDs show very less cytotoxicity on both MDCK (97%) and HeLa cells (96%) even though in presence of high concentration (25μL). Therefore the cytotoxicity results revealed that synthesized N-CDs shows very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility against both MDCK and HeLa cells. The fluorescent N-CDs are ideal cell imaging probes with minimal cytotoxicity [55]. The cellular uptakes of the N-CDs were analyzed by laser scanning confocal microscopy and are depicted in Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 for the MDCK and HeLa cells, respectively. From the images, it can be seen that, MDCK and HeLa cells incubated with N-CDs could be observed clearly in all images. The overlaid images demonstrate the ability of the N-CDs to penetrate the cell walls of MDCK and HeLa cells. Thus, the synthesized N-CDs were successfully used as a staining agent for cell-imaging.

A simple microwave assisted approach was demonstrated for the synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs were graphitized with an interlayer distance of 0.21nm and were confirmed by HR-TEM. Nitrogen doping and surface functionalization with amine groups of BA were confirmed by XPS and FT-IR spectroscopic analyses. The D:G intensity ratio (ID <IG) was 0.77, which revealed the graphitation of synthesized N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs emitted highly fluorescent blue light in the visible range (400–450nm) with excitation at different wavelengths. The calculated quantum yield for the synthesized N-CDs is about 14% against the reference quinine sulfate. The higher quantum yield of N-CDs is might be due to doping of nitrogen over the surface of N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs were showed minimal cytotoxicity on both MDCK and HeLa cells. Further, the N-CDs were successfully utilized as a staining probe for the confocal cell imaging of MDCK and HeLa cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIP) (NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391) and Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).
